creator: marklmi26-fbsd@yahoo.com
text:
(In reply to Matthias Andree from comment #3)

Quoting more explicit material from ISO/IEC 9899:2001 (E). . .

"If the expression that denotes the called function has a type that does not include a prototype, the integer promotions are performed on each argument, and arguments [material about float]. These are called the default argument promotions."

"If the function is defined with a type that does not include a prototype, and the types of the arguments after promotion are not compatible with those of the parameters after promotion, the behavior is undefined, except for the following cases:

-- one promoted type is a singed Integer type, the other promoted type is the corresponding unsigned integer type, , and the value is representable in both types;

-- both types are pointers to qualified or unqualified versions of a character type or void."

"If the expression that denotes the called function has a type that does include a prototype, the arguments are implicitly converted, as if by assignment, to the types of the corresponding parameters, taking the type of each parameter to be the unqualified version of its declared type."

End quote.

That last is what the calls fit under given the (forward) declaration that is present. Then the prototype vs. definition compatibility text quoted earlier gives sufficient context for that to work with the older K&R style definition.

Quoting again: "If one type has a parameter type list and the other type is specified by a function definition that contains a (possibly empty) identifier list, both shall agree in the number of parameters, and the type of each prototype parameter shall be compatible with the the type that results from the application of the default argument promotions to the type of the corresponding identifier."
